pages.text: One clear evening, <name> sat in the backyard, staring at the twinkling night sky. The stars shimmered like diamonds, and the Moon glowed softly. “I wonder how the planets move around the Sun,” he whispered.
His friend Aarav walked by, squinting at the sky. “Why do they go in circles? Can’t they just float anywhere?” he asked.
<name> scratched his head. He wanted to explain, but he wasn’t sure himself. The question puzzled him so much that he decided to find out the truth.

The next day, <name> visited the local observatory. A friendly astronomer welcomed him and showed him a telescope. “Look closely,” she said. Through the lens, <name> could see Mercury, Venus, and Earth moving gracefully along their paths.

“Why don’t they float away?” he asked. “Gravity,” the astronomer explained. “The Sun’s gravity pulls the planets toward it, and their forward motion keeps them from falling straight in. This balance makes them orbit the Sun in paths we call ellipses.”

<name> was fascinated. He sketched the Sun in the middle and drew curved paths for each planet, labeling Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, and the others. He even tried spinning a ball around a lamp to see how gravity works in action.

Later, he gathered his friends in the backyard. “Aarav,” he said excitedly, “planets don’t just float anywhere! The Sun’s gravity pulls them in, and because they move forward, they circle the Sun instead of flying away. That’s why we see them dancing around in space.”

His friends leaned closer, amazed. “So gravity keeps them on track?” one asked. “Yes!” <name> replied. “And each planet moves at a speed that keeps it in orbit. Too slow, and it would fall into the Sun. Too fast, and it would escape into space.”

That night, <name> looked up again, imagining the planets gliding along invisible tracks around the Sun. He felt a thrill knowing the science behind their motion. Space seemed less mysterious and more like a grand cosmic dance, choreographed by gravity.

<name> realized that curiosity, observation, and asking questions could uncover amazing truths about the universe. He couldn’t wait to learn more about stars, moons, and distant planets in the galaxies beyond.
